title: Black Coffee on Wool
stainChemistry: Black coffee's chlorogenic acid and melanoidin polymers hydrogen-bond and form hydrophobic interactions with wool's keratin. Dan Eisen classifies coffee as a tannin stain that is 'more difficult' on natural fibers like wool. Coffee rings form because the driest portion on the outside picks up more residue as it dries — oxidation darkens the stain as oxygen combines with the tannin, like a cut apple browning. On wool, the narrow pH window (5.5–8.0) and low heat tolerance (30°C) severely limit treatment options. No enzymes, no alkali, no chlorine, no sodium percarbonate — wool protein fiber is as restrictive as silk but with felting risk added.
whyThisWorks: Cold water flush at every step keeps wool below its 30°C felting threshold. Acetic acid (Tannin-Go diluted) works within wool's preferred slightly-acidic pH range to disrupt the chlorogenic acid-keratin hydrogen bonds. Multiple gentle acid cycles replace the aggressive oxidizer step that wool can't safely tolerate. Dan Eisen's sequence of 'vinegar and detergent → rinse → peroxide (tested) → vinegar neutralize' applies, but on wool the peroxide step must use only 3% at cool temperature with no ammonia. The vinegar neutralization step is critical — it returns wool to its preferred acidic pH and prevents any alkaline yellowing.

spottingProtocol.technique: Blot with cold water — no rubbing, no agitation
spottingProtocol.temperature: Cold (60–65°F) — above 86°F (30°C) opens wool scales and risks felting
spottingProtocol.instruction: Blot coffee immediately with clean white cloth. Place wool face-down on towel, spray cold water from back. Blot gently — wool is 25% weaker wet. No agitation, no rubbing. Dan Eisen: coffee on the fabric 'picks up oxygen from the air causing it to darken' — speed matters. The cold flush removes dissolved tannin before oxidation begins.

spottingProtocol.agent: A.L. Wilson Tannin-Go (diluted 1:2 with cold water for wool)
spottingProtocol.technique: Cotton pad application, light tamping only — no brushing wool
spottingProtocol.temperature: Ambient — no steam on wool during tannin treatment
spottingProtocol.dwellTime: 2–4 minutes per cycle, up to 3 cycles
spottingProtocol.instruction: Dilute Tannin-Go 1:2. Apply with cotton pad using light tamping. Never brush wet wool — mechanical action on open cuticle scales causes permanent felting. The acid disrupts chlorogenic acid's hydrogen bonds to keratin. Flush with cold water after each cycle. Dan Eisen's approach: 'Apply vinegar and detergent, rub area, rinse' — on wool, modify 'rub' to 'tamp gently.' Multiple mild cycles are safer than one aggressive treatment.

spottingProtocol.technique: Surfactant-assisted acid treatment for melanoidin polymers
spottingProtocol.instruction: Mix 2 drops NSD into white vinegar diluted 1:2 with cold water. Apply with cotton pad. The surfactant helps emulsify melanoidin browning products that resist acid alone. Dan Eisen uses 'detergent mixed with water plus a little distilled vinegar' — the NSD is the professional equivalent. Tamp gently. Flush with cold water.

spottingProtocol.agent: Hydrogen peroxide 3% (maximum safe for wool — cool, no catalyst)
spottingProtocol.technique: Brief contact oxidation for residual melanoidin browning
spottingProtocol.temperature: Cool — NO heat activation on wool, absolutely NO ammonia
spottingProtocol.instruction: Only if brown staining persists after acid cycles. Apply 3% peroxide only — wool is C-rated for peroxide at 3% max. NO ammonia catalyst (ammonia opens wool scales AND is above wool's pH ceiling). NO heat activation. Apply with cotton swab to stain only. Dan Eisen: 'Apply peroxide, apply ammonia, let sit 3 minutes' — on WOOL, skip the ammonia entirely. Flush with cold water. Apply vinegar immediately after to restore acidic pH.

Essential after any peroxide application on wool. Apply diluted vinegar to restore wool to its preferred slightly-acidic state (pH 5.5–7.0). Dan Eisen: 'Apply vinegar, rinse area.' Flush with cold water. Blot between towels — press gently, never wring. Reshape and air dry flat.

homeSolutions: Fresh spill: Blot immediately, flush with cold water only. Mix white vinegar 1:1 with cold water, blot onto stain gently for 2 minutes, rinse cold. Never rub wool. Repeat vinegar treatment once if needed.

Set stain: Extended cold vinegar soak — submerge in cold water with 2 tablespoons white vinegar per quart for 30 minutes. Blot. Apply single round of 3% peroxide to stain area, wait 3 minutes, rinse. Follow with vinegar rinse. Do NOT use OxiClean on wool.

When to stop: If melanoidin browning persists after vinegar treatment + one peroxide application, stop. Wool cannot tolerate repeated oxidizer exposure or any aggressive chemistry. Take to a dry cleaner — specify it's coffee on wool, no enzymes or alkali were used.
materialWarnings: NEVER use protease enzymes on wool — wool IS a protein fiber (keratin); protease digests the fiber
NEVER use chlorine bleach — destroys keratin protein and causes irreversible yellowing
NEVER use sodium percarbonate (OxiClean) — oxygen release + alkalinity is rated F (NEVER) for wool
NEVER use hot water or steam — opens wool cuticle scales causing felting; also sets tannin permanently
NEVER use ammonia — alkaline pH opens scales AND exceeds wool's pH ceiling of 8.0
NEVER rub or brush wet wool — mechanical action + open scales = irreversible felting
NEVER agitate in washing machine — use hand-wash or professional cleaning only

professional.name: A.L. Wilson Tannin-Go
professional.use: Diluted 1:2 for wool — primary chlorogenic acid bond breaker
professional.note: Acetic acid stays within wool's safe pH range. Multiple mild cycles preferred.

professional.name: Street's Tannin Remover
professional.use: Alternative tannin spotter — dilute 1:3 for wool
professional.note: Test on hidden area — some wool dyes may be acid-sensitive

professional.name: Hydrogen Peroxide 3%
professional.use: Brief contact for melanoidin browning — no catalyst, no heat
professional.note: C-rated for wool — use once only, follow with vinegar to restore pH

professional.name: NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent)
professional.note: pH-neutral surfactant is safe for wool — nonionic formulation preferred

consumer.name: White distilled vinegar (5% acetic acid)
consumer.use: Dilute 1:1 with cold water — primary tannin spotter for wool
consumer.note: Safest effective tannin treatment; within wool's preferred acidic range

consumer.name: Hydrogen Peroxide 3%
consumer.use: Last resort for brown melanoidin — test first, one application
consumer.note: Follow immediately with vinegar rinse. No repeat applications on wool.

consumer.name: Woolite (or wool-specific detergent)
consumer.note: pH-balanced for wool. Cold water, gentle handling, flat dry.

escalation.when: Brown melanoidin persists after three acid cycles + one peroxide application, or wool shows texture/felting change during treatment
escalation.whatToTell: This is coffee melanoidin browning on wool. I kept everything cold, acidic, and enzyme-free. No alkali, no chlorine, no OxiClean. Used only diluted tannin spotter and a single 3% peroxide application. The fiber is undamaged but the stain chemistry has exceeded home treatment capability.
escalation.specialistType: Dry cleaner with GreenEarth or hydrocarbon solvent — both A-rated for wool. Avoid PERC if possible (C-rated, may felt if residual moisture present). Ask for wet-side tannin pre-spot before the cleaning cycle.

solventNote: Pre-spot wet side with diluted tannin formula before dry cleaning — coffee tannin sets permanently at machine temperature (140–150°F). GreenEarth preferred for wool — A-rated with no shrinkage risk. Hydrocarbon also A-rated. PERC is C-rated (may cause felting if moisture present). Solvon K4 is A-rated if available. For set stains: professional tannin formula with extended dwell, followed by controlled 3% peroxide with no ammonia. Level with Sofspot before machine load.
